speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? This is the granddaughter of Ivan Chaichuk, from Rylovka. And she's a druggist. And her name is Svetlana. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the picture was made in 1985. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know the date. Place, Rylovka. In Rylovka, village, Rylovka. Her mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother was Maria. Maria. I don't know her last name. The mother's name is Maria. And the the grand-- grandfather's name, Ivan Chaichuk. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is the picture of Maria's daughter, Tanya. The picture was taken-- took place in Rylovka. And the date is around '89, 1989. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the picture, what we saw before, on the right side, from Maria, her daughter, younger daughter. Her name is Tanya. And she's a pharmacist too. Married, with two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Maria was-- her mother was the one who was four years old, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Her mother-- when I was in the hiding place, Maria was-- her mother was four years old. I played with her-- little girl. This is Maria's older daughter, Svetlana, we saw it before, her, and her little girl, 2 years old. The picture was taken in Kiev. Because she lives near Kiev. And the date is '94. I received a Christmas card in '94, and this-- with that picture. And I want to tell you a little story about this little girl, where she is now. She was childless for 10 years. And she wrote me a letter, maybe I can help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here was a lot of Russian immigrants. And I had a girl, a woman, was-- well, she's a doctor. And I talked to her. And I told her the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I have a doctor in Russia. And she gave me the doctor from Russia. She went to the doctor, and-- woman doctor. And through that woman-- through that doctor, she gave birth to the first baby. She was 12 years childless. Now she has a little girl, 2 years old, thanks to me. Svetlana-- and the name of the baby, I don't know. That is written down. Sorry. This is Maria's daughter, and her daughter. And the name of the-- of the little girl is Yarina [INAUDIBLE]. And thanks to me, she has, now, a daughter. Because I connected her with a doctor in Russia, from here. OK, this is the picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maid--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me--

speaker: interviewee
text: The name--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Nathan, and-- Mr. And Mrs. Nathan Skaletzky. And the picture is made in Eschwege, by Kassel, Germany. The date of the-- of the picture is in 1946. 1946, Eschwege, by Kassel, Germany. West Germany. That's all. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you elaborate, this is you and your husband. I said, that's the-- that is Clara Skaletzky and Nathan Skaletzky, myself and my husband. Pregnant, three months, with my first baby. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my first son, Harry Skaletzky, Roberta, his wife, [? Talia, ?] the older one, and the younger one is [? Renata. ?] They live in Sacramento, California. That's it. What else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry today is how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that picture was made in Sacramento, photo. And the picture was made, I think-- it was-- oh yes, we were in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is Harry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry is born 1948. March 4th, 1948. How old is he now? He's going to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: 46. He's old-- I don't know how old is my daughter in law. She never tells her age. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my grandchildren. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandchildren from my younger son, [? Albert. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The one on the left is Marshall Skaletzky. The middle boy is David Skaletzky. And the little girl is [? Michelle ?] Skaletzky. The picture was made-- [INAUDIBLE], '88, 1988-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --in Chicago, Illinois. That's it. This is a-- this is a picture from Nathan and Clara Skaletzky. And made in Chicago, Illinois, 1957. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's it. This is a family picture from Nathan and Clara Skaletzky and two sons. On the left is Albert. On the right is Harry. Taken-- the picture was taken in Chicago, Illinois, and in 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And what were you doing then? I was-- well, my husband was in the business, concessionaire.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I helped him in the business. OK, [? I fixed ?] one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shh. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the original? being a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, all the pictures are 1957. What was-- he was-- Harry was-- they are three years apart. '48, '57, nine years old. And Albert was six years old, I think. Six years, seven years old. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our older son, Harry Skaletzky. And it's-- take-- the picture was taken in Chicago, Illinois, by my husband, and graduation from high school. [? '48 ?] was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger son, Albert Skaletzky, taken by my husband, the picture, in Chicago, Illinois, on his high school graduation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Date, I don't know the date. The graduation. He was 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: The crew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, what is the date? I don't remember. I have to figure out the date. Can you stop it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: '67, I think. This is-- the next [? three-age ?] picture is-- are my three grandchildren from my younger son, Albert, taken and lately, 1994, in San Diego. That's it. This is my younger son, Herbert's daughter, Michelle. She is 7 and 1/2 years old. And the picture was taken in San Diego, California, in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my two grands-- grandchildren, [? Michelle ?] in the left side, David in the right side, in San Diego, California, taken in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older son, Harry's older daughter, [? Talia ?] Skaletzky. Picture's taken in Sacramento, California, 1992. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the picture from my younger son, Albert. Michelle Skaletzky. Picture was taken in San Diego, California, in 19-- in 1990. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is the picture of my younger daughter-- granddaughter from Harry Skaletzky. Taken-- the picture was taken in Sacramento, in 1990. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her name is [? Renata ?] Skaletzky. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Well, this is my wife, Clara, Nathan, and my-- my son. Albert, come in here. My son, Albert. He is the youngest one. Born in Chicago. In-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): Born in Chicago-- Yeah, 1950, November 21. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we growed up together in the same town, and we went together on the same school. And we knew each other from childhood, and later on in life we married each other. In 1945, November 20, in Poland, Lodz. And we have-- we're almost-- we are 49 years married together. We had a wonderful life, altogether. We raised two children, sent them to college, tried the best. I never told my kids what I went through. Because I didn't want-- want them to know that. It's good that I know by myself. My husband, Nathan, you used to talk to them about everything. Now, they're going to see the tape, and what's going on with me. And-- and-- it-- it-- and also with my husband, what we went through. And we are leaving a message to our children that we have to-- never again. We'll be Jews. And don't let yourself down. Not only Jewish people, all the-- all people should be-- they should live-- live free. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Live together. Live to-- free. NATHAN (HUSBAND): And-- And we should live together and as long as we can. And thank God. He's--

speaker: interviewee
text: NATHAN (HUSBAND): Next year we're going to be at 50 anniversary, for us both. And I hope I'm going to live through-- I went through a sickness. I had that whole-- that-- what do you call it, the sickness?

speaker: interviewee
text: ALBERT (SON): You had a stroke. Stroke. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Stroke. ALBERT (SON): But you-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): So I'm not so perfect. But I still hope that maybe in a year from now I'm going to celebrate a 50 anniversary. No one-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): And I hope I'll live through the 2000 years. It's only five years from now. To celebrate the 2000 years. NATHAN (HUSBAND): I remember my mother use to say she would live through the 900, they made a big celebration. And she was telling me that in her time, 900-- 1900, made a big celebration. So my dream was to live to 2000 and see the celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Albert, tell us about when you were born, and a little bit about your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: ALBERT (SON): I was born [INAUDIBLE] like my parents, in Chicago, in 1950. I was raised in Chicago. Spent most of my-- my time, my childhood in Chicago. I spent a couple of years in Israel. I came back to the-- to the United States. And then most of my adult life was in Chicago, but moved out to California about three years ago. And that's, in a brief, summary of my life. That's what I did. Just wanted to add something for my-- for my parents. Germany. ALBERT (SON): That with the help of Hashem, my father will-- will have, and my mother will have, their-- the 50th anniversary. And he will get his wish also, to get, with the help of Hashem, the year of 2000. So Baruch Hashem for that. My Albert is now, well, he's religious. He became a Chabad, in Israel-- shomer Shabbos. ALBERT (SON): I'm not exactly Chabad, but I'm-- I'm-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): You-- ALBERT (SON): --sort of, I guess, a [INAUDIBLE]. So I-- so he-- he is following the steps of his grandparents. The grandparents was Orthodox. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Great-grandparents. Parent-- Grandparents. ALBERT (SON): Well, the-- the town-- the town that my parents come from, Korzec, is one of the major-- Hasidic. ALBERT (SON): Which I found out just recently, is one of the major Hasidic centers. There's-- out of four or five towns, out of many hundreds, maybe thousands, Korzec is one of the major Hasidic centers. And I didn't even know about it until just recently. So-- which was quite interesting for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know something about each of their fathers that you could tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: ALBERT (SON): Very briefly. I know they were both killed. I know that much, by the-- by the Nazis. And I understand they were also buried in the same mass grave together. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Not mass grave. On the cemetery. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Ah-- ALBERT (SON): Mother's, [? not-- ?] NATHAN (HUSBAND): Not a cemetery. It was in a fortress. We had our own fortress, [INAUDIBLE]. Someone told me the-- I had a hiding place. And they find them a week late-- later, after they-- they kill all the people. They were hiding in that place. The Gestapo, they will find them. A week later they took out my brother, my wife's father, and the uncle. She had an uncle. And the aunt. NATHAN (HUSBAND): And a aunt. All four. And they killed them and-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): And they took-- they took them out there in that fortress over there. And they killed them, I was told. And they were laying together. We don't know where they are buried, actually. NATHAN (HUSBAND): The fortress has a lot of big ground. And I don't know exactly where it is. I know in the fortress, on the fortress-- fortress ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Albert, have you been back to Korzec? To the-- ALBERT (SON): No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: NATHAN (HUSBAND): I was attending there. We had a stroke and I didn't go. ALBERT (SON): There might be something in the future. I think they're doing a-- people from my-- my parents' town, the remnant of the survivors, are planning a second reunion. They did-- they planned and did a successful monument to the Jews who were killed there. But they're going back, I-- I think, in the future, to do-- rebuild the grave site. Because right now it's-- it's run amok. There's like, animals walking all over it. And the-- and the gravestones are turned over. So they're trying to-- This is the main cemetery. ALBERT (SON): --raise some money to go back and to-- The main cemetery. ALBERT (SON): --rebuild the cemetery, which has a history of over 500 years. So if that happens, then if-- I might go. But I really don't have any real feeling to go there. But for that, I'd go for.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you know about your parents' experience growing up? Can you share anything with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: ALBERT (SON): Well, like my mother said, she didn't speak too much about it. My father spoke about it. He had a very difficult life, a very hard life. And then on top of that, when the war broke out, it was even harder because he was the only one of his family to survive. But-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): But my children had a good life. They had, each one, a separate room. We had three bedroom in a two-flat new apartment. Each one had a separate bedroom. And they finish high school in a Jewish neighborhood. There was no fighting there. [INAUDIBLE]. NATHAN (HUSBAND): And at that time I had a real good time with them, the reasons, they had good-- they were a good pupil. And my older son, Harry, is a lawyer. He-- he had good marks. Same thing, Albert. He had good marks, and he finished high school. And-- College. NATHAN (HUSBAND): And then they went for [INAUDIBLE]. One year, first year of college, he was in Chicago. He had good marks, too. Later on, they-- he wants to be like everybody else. He run away to Vermont, to the college. And over there, he did learn no trade, no special trade. He waste his time a little bit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a final thought that you would like to share with the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't let yourself down. Keep your head up. And never again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All people, they all-- everybody. And keep yourself free-- NATHAN (HUSBAND): And people have to be organized. --in a country. NATHAN (HUSBAND): They have be a government. They have to listen to the government. And you have to organize. If you're not organized, and you don't listen to the government, you lost. And that's-- that's why-- but the Jewish people were lost in the war. Because it was not organized. And-- and that's what happens. All kinds of bad things about the Jews, because they was not organized. That's all, that's the message. NATHAN (HUSBAND): Now, I hope in the future, you're going to be organized. And you're going to listen. We are-- we're praying for peace for the whole world, and peace in Israel. That's what I'm for, praying. And everybody in every nation should be free, not to be discriminated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all would be the message. Why don't you give the message, Albert?

speaker: interviewee
text: ALBERT (SON): The-- this testimony to my parents, that the-- that the revisionists should know that the Holocaust happened. And whatever they say, they-- my parents and many thousands of others must know that there was a terrible Holocaust. And whatever they say is just lies. And I'm glad that we-- my parents decided to do this today. So for the future, they will know that-- that-- It was real. ALBERT (SON): It happened. It was a horrible nightmare. But it happened. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great. ALBERT (SON): OK. Thank you. Are your knees OK? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Hey, that's all.
